elements.sent-2411-001: In this article the weakest possible theorem giving a foundation behind the Hilbert space formalism of quantum theory is stated.
elements.sent-2411-002: The necessary postulates are formulated, and the mathematics is spelled out in details.
elements.sent-2411-003: It is argued that, from this approach, a general epistemic interpretation of quantum mechanics is natural.
elements.sent-2411-004: Some applications to the Bell experiment and to decision theory are briefly discussed.
elements.sent-2411-005: The article represents the conclusion of a series of articles and books on quantum foundation.
elements.sent-2411-006: Keywords: Accessible theoretical variables; Bell experiment; decision theory; epistemic interpretation; inaccessible theoretical variables; postulates; quantum foundation.
elements.sent-2411-007: In a number of articles, the newest ones being Helland (2024a,b,c), this author has proposed a completely new foundation of quantum theory, a foundation based upon theoretical variables, which in a given context may be attached to an observer or to a group of communicating observers.
elements.sent-2411-008: These variables can be accessible or inaccessible.
elements.sent-2411-009: An example of a (maximal) accessible variable may be the positions $\bm{q = (\bm{q _1, ...}}
elements.sent-2411-010: A typical inaccessible variable may be the vector $(\bm{q ,\bm{p )$.}}
elements.sent-2411-011: By assuming the existence of two different maximal accessible variables - in Niels Bohr's terminology two complementary variables - and making some additional assumptions, it is shown in the above articles that essentially the whole Hilbert space apparatus results.
elements.sent-2411-012: The purpose of the present article is to look closer at the additional assumptions.
elements.sent-2411-013: It turns out that these can be considerably weakened.
elements.sent-2411-014: What we do have to assume, is that the two variables, called $\theta$ and $\eta$ in the general theory, may be seen as functions of some basic inaccessible variable $\phi$, and that groups act on both $\phi$ and $\theta$.
elements.sent-2411-015: In Helland (2024a) it was assumed that the two actual variables were related ($\eta(\phi)=\theta(k\phi)$ for some transformation $k$ on the space $\Omega_\phi$, the range of $\phi$).
elements.sent-2411-016: It is shown here that we only have to assume: 1) The spaces $\Omega_\theta$ and $\Omega_\eta$ have the same category; 2) There is a group $M$ acting on $\Omega_\phi$.
elements.sent-2411-017: In the example above, we can let $M$ be the multivariate Weil-Heisenberg group.
elements.sent-2411-018: We also have to assume that there is a transitive group $G$ acting on $\Omega_\theta$, and that a left-invariant measure $\mu$ with respect to $G$ is given.
elements.sent-2411-019: In the above example we can just let $G$ be the translation group.
elements.sent-2411-020: In Helland (2024a) it was assumed that a multivariate representation $U(\cdot)$ with certain properties existed.
elements.sent-2411-021: In the present paper we show how a simple such representation can be constructed.
elements.sent-2411-022: This assumption is simply not necessary.
elements.sent-2411-023: From this, a weakest possible version of my main theorem is given as Theorem 1 below.
elements.sent-2411-024: The conclusion of the theorem is that every accessible variable has a symmetric operator in $\mathcal{H =L^2 (\Omega_\psi, \nu )$ attached to it, where $\psi=(\theta,\eta)$, and $\nu$ is an invariant measure on $\Omega_\psi$ induced by the invariant measure $\mu$ on $\Omega_\theta$.}
elements.sent-2411-025: This is the starting point for much of the Hilbert space apparatus.
elements.sent-2411-026: And apart from the above symmetry assumption, the essential assumption is only the existence of two complementary theoretical variables in the given context.
elements.sent-2411-027: Note that there are no macroscopic assumptions here.
elements.sent-2411-028: Thus this derivation also gives a foundation of what Khrennikov (2010, 2023) calls quantum-like models.
elements.sent-2411-029: These models have links to several scientific disciplines.
elements.sent-2411-030: The link to quantum decision theory will be discussed elsewhere.
elements.sent-2411-031: Links to relativity theory and to quantum field theory are discussed in Helland (2023c) and in Helland and Parthasarathy (2024).
elements.sent-2411-032: I will concentrate on the derivation of the Hilbert space apparatus and related derivations in this article.
elements.sent-2411-033: Assumptions that lead to the Born rule for probabilities are discussed, and the derivation is proved, in Helland (2024b).
elements.sent-2411-034: Derivation of the Schr\"{o dinger equation from a few postulates has been given for instance by Klein (2010).}
elements.sent-2411-035: Finally, it is a basic setting that the theoretical variables are attached to an observer or to a group of communicating observers.
elements.sent-2411-036: A natural assumption in addition is that they are connected to the mind(s) ot this/these observer(s).
elements.sent-2411-037: This leads to an epistemic interpretation of quantum theory, an interpretation that contains QBism (see for instance Fuchs et al., 2013) as a special case.
elements.sent-2411-038: Quantum theory is seen as a theory of our knowledge about the real world, not directly about the real world.
elements.sent-2411-039: The plan of this article is as follows: In Section 2 the theory is outlined in its weakest possible version.
elements.sent-2411-040: In Section 3 some consequences, consequences to an understanding of the Bell theorem and to a new theory of decisions, are briefly discussed.
elements.sent-2411-041: In Section 4 some final remarks are given.
elements.sent-2411-042: I repeat that my main notion is that of theoretical variables, which can be almost anything.
elements.sent-2411-043: The theoretical variables can be accessible or inaccessible.
elements.sent-2411-044: From a mathematical point of view I only assume that if $\lambda$ is a theoretical variable and $\theta$ is a fixed function of $\lambda$, then $\theta$ is a theoretical variable.
elements.sent-2411-045: And if $\lambda$ is accessible, then $\theta$ is accessible.
elements.sent-2411-046: For physical modelling I assume a fixed context, and that an observer or a group of communicating observers in this context has/have a set of theoretical variables associated with him/them.
elements.sent-2411-047: In the case of a group of observers, their communication should be related to these variables.
elements.sent-2411-048: Then my first postulate is as follows: Postulate 1 There is an inaccessible variable $\phi$ such that all accessible variables can be seen as functions of $\phi$.
elements.sent-2411-049: There is a group $M$ acting on $\Omega_\phi$.
elements.sent-2411-050: In simple physical examples, such a $\phi$ can easily be constructed.
elements.sent-2411-051: As a general statement covering all possible situation, Postulate 1 can also be given a religious interpretation, see Helland (2022d, 2023d).
elements.sent-2411-052: One possible option is to replace Postulate 1 with some assumptions in category theory; see the arXiv version of Helland (2024a).
elements.sent-2411-053: Category theory in the foundation of quantum mechanics has also been considered by others, for instance Coecke and Papette (2009) or Döring and Isham (2008).
elements.sent-2411-054: This option will not be considered further in the present article.
elements.sent-2411-055: My main theorems will refer to a situation where we have two different maximal accessible theoretical variables, which I, following Niels Bohr, will call two complementary variables.
elements.sent-2411-056: I will show that the whole Hilbert space apparatus follows under weak conditions from the assumption that we have two such maximal accessible variables.
elements.sent-2411-057: The term `maximal' means roughly that the variable cannot be extended and still be accessible.
elements.sent-2411-058: To be precise, I need to define a partial ordering among the variables.
elements.sent-2411-059: Definition 1 Say that $\theta\le\lambda$ if $\theta = f(\lambda)$ for some function $f$.
elements.sent-2411-060: This is a partial ordering among all theoretical variables, and also a partial ordering among the accessible ones.
elements.sent-2411-061: Note that $\phi$ from Postulate 1 is an upper bound in the accessible case.
elements.sent-2411-062: We will say that $\theta$ is a maximal accessible variable if it is maximal with respect to this partial ordering.
elements.sent-2411-063: By Zorn's Lemma, which is equivalent to the Axiom of Choice, maximal variables always exist.
elements.sent-2411-064: For those who do not believe in Zorn's Lemma, we add an additional postulate.
elements.sent-2411-065: Postulate 2 For every accessible variable $\xi$ there is a maximal accessible variable $\theta$ such that $\xi\le\theta$.
elements.sent-2411-066: In order to achieve a meaningful theory, we also need some symmetry assumptions.
elements.sent-2411-067: One such is given by the existence of a group $M$ acting upon $\phi$ (Postulate 1).
elements.sent-2411-068: Another assumption is given by the existence of a group $G$ acting upon $\theta$.
elements.sent-2411-069: In concrete examples, these groups can be easily constructed.
elements.sent-2411-070: Postulate 3 To a given accessible theoretical variable $\theta$ there is a group $G$ acting upon $\theta$, and there is a left-invariant measure $\mu$ with respect to $G$ on $\Omega_\theta$, the range space of $\theta$.
elements.sent-2411-071: The group $G$ is transitive and has a trivial isotropy group.
elements.sent-2411-072: Conditions for the existence of an invariant measure are discussed in Helland (2021).
elements.sent-2411-073: Note that if an invariant measure is supposed to act on each single theoretical variable, there is also from this an invariant measure on every set of theoretical variables.
elements.sent-2411-074: Finally, in this article I will assume for two complementary variables $\theta$ and $\eta$:: Postulate 4 The range space $\Omega_\theta$ is either finite and has the same number of values as $\Omega_\eta$, or, more generally, $\Omega_\theta$ and $\Omega_\eta$ have the same category.
elements.sent-2411-075: More intuitively, it means that there is a bijective function connecting $\Omega_\theta$ and $\Omega_\eta$.) This is all we need for our first results, a Proposition and a basic Theorem.
elements.sent-2411-076: Proposition 1 Assume that the basic inaccessible variable $\phi$ satisfies Postulate 1, and that two given accessible variables $\theta$ and $\eta$ satisfy Postulate 4.
elements.sent-2411-077: Then $\theta$ and $\eta$ are either in one-to-one correspondence, or the following holds: There exists an accessible variable $\xi$ which is a bijective function of $\eta$, a transformation $k$ in $\Omega_\phi$, and a function $f$ acting on $\Omega_\phi$ such that $\theta = f(\phi)$ and $\xi = f(k\phi)$.
elements.sent-2411-078: In many applications, it turns out that this will hold with $\xi$ equal to $\eta$.
elements.sent-2411-079: In that case we say that $\theta$ and $\eta$ are related: $\theta=f(\phi)$ and $\eta=f(k\phi)$ for some $k$.
elements.sent-2411-080: Proof The finite-dimentional case was treated in Section 7.2 of Helland (2024a), so I will here look at the more general case.

elements.sent-2411-082: Let $\phi_2$ be any point in $\Omega_\phi$ such that $\eta(\phi_1)=f(\phi_2)$.

elements.sent-2411-109: In an ordinary physical setting, it is natural to interpret the accessible theoretical variables as ordinary physical variables, but also connected to the mind of an observer or to the joint minds of a communicating group of observers.
elements.sent-2411-110: This gives a general epistemic interpretion of quantum theory, an interpretation which has QBism as a special case.

Proof See Theorem 5 of Helland (2024a). $\Box$ I should also mention again the consequences for accessible theoretical variables that take a discrete set of values. - Every accessible variable has a symmetric operator associated to it. - The set of eigenvalues of an operator is equal to the possible values of the variable. - An accessible variable is maximal if and only if all eigenvalues are simple. - The eigenvectors can, in the maximal case, be interpreted in terms of a question together with an answer. Specifically, it means in a context with several variables, a chosen variable $\theta$ may be associated with a question `What is the value of $\theta$?' or `What will $\theta$ be if we measure it?', and a specific eigenvector of $A^\theta$, corresponding to the eigenvalue $u$ may be identified with the answer `$\theta=u$. - In the general case, eigenspaces have the same interpretation. - The operators of related variables are connected by a unitary similarity transform. It should also be mentioned that, in addition to the Postulates of this article and of Helland (2024b,c), a final postulate is needed to compute probabiliies of independent events. A version of such a postulate is Postulate 5 \textit{If the probability of an event $E_1$ is computed by a probability amplitude $z_1$ from the Born rule in the Hilbert space $H_1$, the probability of an event $E_2$ is computed by a probability amplitude $z_2$ from the Born rule in the Hilbert space $H_2$, and these two events are independent, then the probability of the event $E_1 \cap E_2$ can be computed from the probanility amplitude $z_1z_2$, associated with the Hilbert space $H_1\otimesH_2$.} This postulate can be motivated by its relation to classical probability theory: If $P(E_1)=|z_1|^2$ and $P(E_2)=|z_2|^2$, then \[ P(E_1\cap E_2)=P(E_1)P(E_2)=|z_1|^2 |z_2|^2 = |z_1z_2]^2.\end{equation}.

elements.section-2411-003: A mathematical consequence and an interpretation
elements.sent-2411-140: This section has been included to illustrate an important aspect of my theory: From purely mathematical postulates and theorems, and by just adding a natural interpretation, the following conclusions are derived: 1) general psychological statements having universal validity; 2) an explanation of a physical phenomenon which has been verified empirically, but which otherwise seems to be difficult to understand.
elements.sent-2411-141: The theme of this section has been treated in previous articles, and the mathematical proofs are defered to these articles.
elements.sent-2411-142: However, the above aspect of the theory has not been clearly stressed before.
elements.sent-2411-143: The property of being related ($\eta(\phi)=\theta(k\phi)$ for some $k$), is an important relation between two maximal accessible theoretical variables $\theta$ and $\eta$.
elements.sent-2411-144: By Theorem 2, if $\theta$ and $\eta$ are related, there is a unitary similarity transformation between the corresponding operators.
elements.sent-2411-145: This theorem has an inverse for finite-dimensional variables.
elements.sent-2411-146: Theorem 4 Consider two maximal accessible finite-dimensional theoretical variables $\theta$ and $\eta$.
elements.sent-2411-147: If there is a transformation $k$ in $\Omega_\phi$ and a unitary transformation $W(k)$ such that $A^\eta = W(k)^\dagger A^\theta W(k)$, then $\theta$ and $\eta$ are related.
elements.sent-2411-148: The theorem then follows from the Lemma of Section 3 in Helland (2023b).
elements.sent-2411-149: Concentrate here on the first case, and call the observer $O$.
elements.sent-2411-150: Then we have Theorem 5 Assume that two finite-dimensional related maximal accessible $\theta$ and $\eta$ are associated with $O$ in some fixed context.
elements.sent-2411-151: Then $O$ cannot in the same context be associated with another maximal accessible variable $\lambda$ which is related to $\theta$, but not related to $\eta$.
elements.sent-2411-152: Proof This is a consequence of Theorem 4.
elements.sent-2411-153: See the proof of Theorem 1 in Helland (2023b).
elements.sent-2411-154: In my interpretation of quantum theory I connect the variables to the mind of an observer or to the joint minds of a communicating group of observers.
elements.sent-2411-155: Then we can consider maximal observations done in some fixed context, which also means some fixed time.
elements.sent-2411-156: Corollary 1 Assume that $O$ has two related finite-dimensional maximal accessible variables $\theta$ and $\eta$ in his mind at some fixed time $t$.
elements.sent-2411-157: Then he cannot simultaneous have in his mind another maximal accessible variable that is related to $\theta$, but not related to $\eta$.
elements.sent-2411-158: It is crucial here that time is fixed.
elements.sent-2411-159: By letting time vary, $O$ is able to think of many variables, also unrelated ones.
elements.sent-2411-160: In Helland (2022b, 2023b) this conclusion is applied to the observer Charlie, which observes the results of Alice and Bob in the famous Bell experiment.
elements.sent-2411-161: It is concluded that, from this statement it is possible to understand that in practice, noting that Charlie can be any observer, the violation of the CHSH inequality in practice can be understood.
elements.sent-2411-162: Note that my conclusion here is not directly a consequence of quantum mechanics, but of a series of mathematical theorems, building upon the above 4 postulates.
elements.sent-2411-163: Another application is to decision theory.
elements.sent-2411-164: Let $O$ be faced with deciding among a finite number of actions $a_1, .... ,a_r$.
elements.sent-2411-165: Define the decision variable $\theta$ as equal to $j$ if the action $a_j$ is chosen ($j=1, .... ,r$).
elements.sent-2411-166: Say that $\theta$ is accessible, and that the decision is accessible, if the decision can be carried out by $O$.
elements.sent-2411-167: The variable $\theta$ is called maximal accessible if the decision just can be carried out.
elements.sent-2411-168: Note that $\theta$ is finite-valued, so the theory of this article applies.
elements.sent-2411-169: Corollary 2 Assume that $O$ at some fixed time $t$ has in his mind two related maximal decisions.
elements.sent-2411-170: Then he is not able to, at the same time think of another decision, which is related to the first of the two decisions, but not related to the second one.
elements.sent-2411-171: Note that here, $O$ can be any person.
elements.sent-2411-172: By my theory we all have this limitation in our minds.
elements.sent-2411-173: The conclusion can also be generalized to the decisions made by a communicationg group of persons.

elements.sent-2411-292: The basis for nearly all statistical inference is a statistical model, a model for the data $z$ in terms of the total parameter $\theta$.
elements.sent-2411-293: This is expressed by a probability function $p(z|\theta)$, in the discrete case a point probability, and in the continuous case a probablity density.
elements.sent-2411-294: The likelihood of the data is defined as $L(\theta |z)=p(z|\theta )$, the probability function seen as a function of the parameter.
elements.sent-2411-295: The following principle can be taken as the basis for very much statsitical inference, and in my theory it is also used as a motivation behind Born's formula.. \begin{equation}\] The generalized likelihood principle Consider two experiments in the same context $\tau$, and assume that $\theta$ is the same full parameter in both experiments. Suppose that two observations $z_1$ and $z_2$ have proportional likelihoods in the two experiments, where the proportionality constant $c$ is independent of $\theta$. Then, these two observations produce the same experimental evidence on $\theta$ in this context. The term ``experimental evidence'' is here left undefined and can be specified in any desirable direction. In some statistical textbooks and articles, the likelihood principle is formulated without specifying a context. As discussed in Helland (2021, 2024b), specifying $\tau$ implies that the principle becomes much less controversial. One may also assume that the two contexts are similar, i.e., one-to-one functions of each other. The second necessary postulate is Postulate 7 Consider a physical context $\tau$ observed by an observer $B$ whose decisions are influenced by a superior actor $D$. Assume that $D$'s probabilities are taken as experimental evidence and that $D$ is seen by $B$ to be rational in agreement with the Dutch Book Principle. The Dutch Book Principle No choice of payoffs in a series of bets shall lead to a sure loss for the bettor. From these two postulates, assuming perfect experiments in the sense that no experimental noise is assumed, and using a version of Gleason's theorem given by Busch (2003), the following versions of Born's formula is proved. Consider two experiments with maximal accessible discrete parameters $\theta^a$ and $\theta^b$, having the same number of values. By a corollary to Theorem 1 (Helland, 2024a), there are operators $A^a$ and $A^b$ in a Hilbert space $H$ associated with these two parameters, the eigenvalues of the operators are the possible values of the parameters, and the corresponding normed eigenvectors are simple. Let $|a;k\rangle$ correspond to the event $\theta^a = u_k$ and $|b;j\rangle$ correspond to the event $\theta^b =v_j$. Theorem 4 The conditional probabilities are given by \[ P(\theta^b = v_j |\theta^a = u_k ) = |\langle a;k |b:j\rangle |^2 .\end{equation}.

By a corollary to Theorem 1 (Helland, 2024a), there are operators $A^a$ and $A^b$ in a Hilbert space $H$ associated with these two parameters, the eigenvalues of the operators are the possible values of the parameters, and the corresponding normed eigenvectors are simple. Let $|a;k\rangle$ correspond to the event $\theta^a = u_k$ and $|b;j\rangle$ correspond to the event $\theta^b =v_j$. Theorem 4 The conditional probabilities are given by \[ P(\theta^b = v_j |\theta^a = u_k ) = |\langle a;k |b:j\rangle |^2 .\end{equation}
